title: Find kth smallest in sorted matrix
description: Find kth smallest element in row-wise sorted matrix

examples.explanation: Step 1: Flatten matrix. Iterate rows: row 0: [1,5,9] → add 1,5,9; row 1: [10,11,13] → add 10,11,13; row 2: [12,13,15] → add 12,13,15. Flattened list = [1,5,9,10,11,13,12,13,15]. Step 2: Sort flattened list. Sort [1,5,9,10,11,13,12,13,15] → [1,5,9,10,11,12,13,13,15]. Step 3: Return kth element (k=8, 1-indexed). Access index 7 (8-1) in sorted list: element at index 7 is 13. Output: 13.

Step 1: Flatten matrix. Only one row with one element: [-5]. Flattened list = [-5]. Step 2: Sort flattened list. List with one element remains [-5]. Step 3: Return kth element (k=1). Access index 0 (1-1): element is -5. Output: -5.

approaches.name: Brute Force: Flatten and Sort
approaches.summary: Flatten the matrix into a 1D array, sort it, and return the kth element. This is straightforward but inefficient for large matrices.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find kth Smallest Element in Row-wise Sorted Matrix Using Flatten and Sort
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of flattening matrix to 1D array, sorting, and returning kth element.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the problem by treating the 2D matrix as a collection of elements that need to be ordered. The intuition is simple: if we can collect all elements into a single list and sort them, the kth smallest element will be at position k-1 in the sorted list (0-indexed). This works because sorting arranges elements in ascending order, making positional access straightforward. The algorithm has three main steps: 1) Flatten the matrix by iterating through all rows and columns, appending each element to a list. This requires nested loops over m rows and n columns. 2) Sort the flattened list using an efficient sorting algorithm like quicksort or timsort. 3) Return the element at index k-1. The time complexity is O(m*n log(m*n)) because flattening takes O(m*n) and sorting takes O(N log N) where N = m*n. Space complexity is O(m*n) for storing the flattened array. This approach is straightforward but inefficient for large matrices due to the sorting overhead and extra space. Connection to real-world scenarios: This mirrors how you might manually solve the problem by writing all numbers on paper, sorting them, and picking the kth. Common mistakes include forgetting that k is 1-indexed (requiring k-1 access), not handling edge cases like k=1 or k=m*n, and inefficient flattening using extra loops. The pseudocode pattern: def kthSmallest(matrix, k): flattened = [] for row in matrix: for element in row: flattened.append(element) flattened.sort() return flattened[k-1].

examples.trace: Step 1: Flatten matrix. Iterate rows: row 0: [1,5,9] → add 1,5,9; row 1: [10,11,13] → add 10,11,13; row 2: [12,13,15] → add 12,13,15. Flattened list = [1,5,9,10,11,13,12,13,15]. Step 2: Sort flattened list. Sort [1,5,9,10,11,13,12,13,15] → [1,5,9,10,11,12,13,13,15]. Step 3: Return kth element (k=8, 1-indexed). Access index 7 (8-1) in sorted list: element at index 7 is 13. Output: 13.

Step 1: Flatten matrix. Only one row with one element: [-5]. Flattened list = [-5]. Step 2: Sort flattened list. List with one element remains [-5]. Step 3: Return kth element (k=1). Access index 0 (1-1): element is -5. Output: -5.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Flatten Matrix into 1D Array
ladders.desc: Convert a 2D matrix into a single list containing all elements.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the first critical step in the brute-force approach. The intuition is to traverse every element in the matrix systematically and collect them into a linear structure. Since a matrix is row-wise sorted (given in the problem), but for flattening, we ignore the sorted property and treat it as a generic 2D array. We need to visit each cell exactly once. The algorithm uses nested loops: an outer loop iterates over each row, and an inner loop iterates over each column in that row. For each element, append it to a list. This ensures all elements are collected in row-major order (left to right, top to bottom). Time complexity is O(m*n) where m is number of rows and n is columns, as we visit m*n elements. Space complexity is O(m*n) for the output list. Connection to L0: This flattened list is then passed to the sorting step. Common mistakes include using inefficient methods like list comprehension without understanding, or incorrect indexing that skips elements. The pseudocode: def flatten(matrix): result = [] for i in range(len(matrix)): for j in range(len(matrix[i])): result.append(matrix[i][j]) return result.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize result = []. Step 2: Outer loop i=0 (first row): row = [1,2,3]. Inner loop j=0: add matrix[0][0]=1 → result=[1]; j=1: add 2 → result=[1,2]; j=2: add 3 → result=[1,2,3]. Step 3: Outer loop i=1 (second row): row = [4,5,6]. Inner loop j=0: add 4 → result=[1,2,3,4]; j=1: add 5 → result=[1,2,3,4,5]; j=2: add 6 → result=[1,2,3,4,5,6]. Output: [1,2,3,4,5,6].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Sort a List to Find kth Smallest Element
ladders.desc: Sort an array and retrieve the element at a specific position (k-1).
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique: after flattening, we sort the list to order elements ascendingly, then directly access the kth smallest via index. The intuition is that sorting transforms an unordered collection into a sequence where the ith position (0-indexed) holds the (i+1)th smallest element. Thus, for 1-indexed k, the target is at index k-1. The algorithm: 1) Use a sorting algorithm (e.g., built-in sort like Python's Timsort) on the list. 2) After sorting, check if k is within valid range (1 to length of list). 3) Return the element at index k-1. Time complexity depends on sorting: O(N log N) for comparison sorts, where N is list length. Space complexity is O(1) for in-place sort or O(N) if using extra space. Connection to L1: This takes the flattened list as input. Common mistakes include not adjusting for 1-indexed k, not handling invalid k, or using inefficient custom sorts. The pseudocode: def kth_from_sorted(arr, k): if k < 1 or k > len(arr): return None arr.sort() return arr[k-1].

examples.trace: Step 1: Input arr = [12,3,5,7,19], k=2. Step 2: Sort arr. Sorting [12,3,5,7,19] → [3,5,7,12,19]. Step 3: k is 1-indexed, so access index 1 (2-1). Element at index 1 is 5. Output: 5.
Step 1: arr = [1,1,1,2,2,3], k=4. Step 2: Sort arr (already sorted): [1,1,1,2,2,3]. Step 3: Access index 3 (4-1). Element at index 3 is 2. Output: 2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterate Through 2D Array with Nested Loops
ladders.desc: Traverse all elements in a matrix using row and column indices.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is essential for flattening: accessing each element in a 2D array requires nested iteration. The intuition is that a matrix can be viewed as a grid with rows and columns; to visit every cell, we use an outer loop for rows and an inner loop for columns. This ensures systematic traversal, typically in row-major order (left to right across each row, then move to next row). The algorithm: for i from 0 to rows-1: for j from 0 to columns-1: access matrix[i][j]. Time complexity is O(rows*columns) as each element is visited once. Space complexity is O(1) extra space if just accessing. Connection to L2: This traversal is used to collect elements into a list before sorting. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop bounds, confusing row and column indices, or using inefficient traversal patterns. The pseudocode: def traverse(matrix): for i in range(len(matrix)): for j in range(len(matrix[i])): # access matrix[i][j].

examples.trace: Step 1: rows=2, columns=2. Step 2: Outer loop i=0 (first row): Inner loop j=0: access matrix[0][0]=1; j=1: access matrix[0][1]=2. Step 3: Outer loop i=1 (second row): Inner loop j=0: access matrix[1][0]=3; j=1: access matrix[1][1]=4. Order of access: 1,2,3,4.
Step 1: rows=1, columns=3. Step 2: Outer loop i=0: Inner loop j=0: access 10; j=1: access 20; j=2: access 30. Order: 10,20,30.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Access Elements in a 2D List Using Indices
ladders.desc: Retrieve a value from a matrix given its row and column position.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is key to working with matrices: accessing an element using two indices. In programming, a 2D list (or array) is often represented as a list of lists, where the first index selects the row and the second selects the column. The intuition is similar to coordinates in a grid: (row, column) pinpoints a specific cell. For example, matrix[i][j] gives the element at row i, column j. It's crucial that indices start at 0 in most languages. The algorithm is straightforward: given matrix, i, j, return matrix[i][j]. Time complexity is O(1) constant time for access. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L3: This access is performed inside nested loops to traverse all elements. Common mistakes include using invalid indices (out of bounds), confusing row and column order, or not understanding 0-indexing. The pseudocode: def access(matrix, i, j): return matrix[i][j].

examples.trace: Step 1: matrix has 2 rows, 3 columns. Step 2: i=0 selects first row: [1,2,3]. Step 3: j=1 selects second element in that row (0-indexed): 2. Output: 2.
Step 1: matrix has 2 rows, 2 columns. Step 2: i=1 selects second row: [30,40]. Step 3: j=0 selects first element in that row: 30. Output: 30.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understand 1D and 2D Lists in Programming
ladders.desc: Learn basic list structures: 1D lists (arrays) and 2D lists (matrices).
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: understanding how data is organized in lists. A 1D list is a linear sequence of elements, like [1,2,3], where each element can be accessed by a single index (e.g., index 0 gives 1). A 2D list is a list of lists, representing a table or grid, like [[1,2],[3,4]], where the first list is the first row and the second list is the second row. To access an element, use two indices: the first picks the row (outer list), the second picks the column (inner list). For example, in [[1,2],[3,4]], matrix[0][1] gives 2. The intuition is that 2D structures help model real-world grids, like spreadsheets. Time and space are basic: accessing is O(1). Connection to L4: This foundation enables element access in matrices. Common mistakes include confusing list dimensions, incorrect indexing, or not initializing lists properly. The pseudocode: # 1D list: my_list = [1,2,3]; element = my_list[0] # gives 1. # 2D list: matrix = [[1,2],[3,4]]; element = matrix[0][1] # gives 2.

examples.trace: Step 1: List is [10,20,30]. Indices: 0→10, 1→20, 2→30. Step 2: Access index 1: element at position 1 is 20. Output: 20.
Step 1: 2D list is [[5,6],[7,8]]. Rows: row 0 is [5,6], row 1 is [7,8]. Step 2: Access row 1 (second row): [7,8]. Step 3: Within that row, access column 0 (first element): 7. Output: 7.

approaches.name: Min-Heap Merge of Sorted Rows
approaches.summary: Use a min-heap to merge the sorted rows, similar to merging k sorted lists, and extract the kth smallest element. This leverages the row-wise sorted property efficiently.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find kth Smallest Element in Row-wise Sorted Matrix Using Min-Heap
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of the min-heap approach to find the kth smallest element in a matrix where each row is sorted.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the kth smallest element in a matrix where each row is sorted in non-decreasing order. The min-heap approach efficiently merges these sorted rows by treating each row as a sorted list. We initialize a min-heap with the first element of each row along with its row and column indices. Then, we repeatedly extract the smallest element from the heap (which is the current smallest overall) and push the next element from the same row into the heap. After k-1 extractions, the next extraction gives the kth smallest element. The intuition is that since rows are sorted, the smallest remaining element is always at the front of some row, and the heap helps us track these efficiently. Time complexity is O(k log m) where m is the number of rows, as we perform k heap operations (each O(log m)). Space complexity is O(m) for the heap. This connects to L1 by using the heap extraction and insertion sub-routine repeatedly. Common mistakes include not handling edge cases like k larger than total elements or empty matrices, and incorrect heap initialization. Real code pattern: Initialize heap with first elements, extract k-1 times while pushing next elements, then extract the kth.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize min-heap with (1,0,0), (10,1,0), (12,2,0). Heap: [(1,0,0), (10,1,0), (12,2,0)].
Step 2: Extract min: 1 (k=1). Push next from row 0: (5,0,1). Heap: [(5,0,1), (10,1,0), (12,2,0)].
Step 3: Extract min: 5 (k=2). Push next from row 0: (9,0,2). Heap: [(9,0,2), (10,1,0), (12,2,0)].
Step 4: Extract min: 9 (k=3). Row 0 exhausted. Heap: [(10,1,0), (12,2,0)].
Step 5: Extract min: 10 (k=4). Push next from row 1: (11,1,1). Heap: [(11,1,1), (12,2,0)].
Step 6: Extract min: 11 (k=5). Push next from row 1: (13,1,2). Heap: [(12,2,0), (13,1,2)].
Step 7: Extract min: 12 (k=6). Push next from row 2: (13,2,1). Heap: [(13,1,2), (13,2,1)].
Step 8: Extract min: 13 from row 1 (k=7). Row 1 exhausted. Heap: [(13,2,1)].
Step 9: Extract min: 13 from row 2 (k=8). This is the 8th smallest. Output: 13.

Step 1: Initialize min-heap with (2,0,0), (3,1,0). Heap: [(2,0,0), (3,1,0)].
Step 2: Extract min: 2 (k=1). Push next from row 0: (6,0,1). Heap: [(3,1,0), (6,0,1)].
Step 3: Extract min: 3 (k=2). Push next from row 1: (7,1,1). Heap: [(6,0,1), (7,1,1)].
Step 4: Extract min: 6 (k=3). This is the 3rd smallest. Output: 6.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Extract Min and Push Next from Same Row Using Min-Heap
ladders.desc: Implement the core operation of extracting the smallest element from a heap and pushing the next element from its row.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the min-heap approach. We maintain a min-heap where each element is a tuple (value, row_index, col_index). The heap is initialized with the first element of each row. In each iteration, we extract the root (smallest element) from the heap. Then, if there is a next element in the same row (i.e., col_index + 1 is within bounds), we push that next element into the heap. The intuition is that after removing the current smallest, the next candidate from that row might become the new smallest, and the heap efficiently maintains order. Time complexity per operation is O(log m) for heap extract and push, where m is heap size (number of rows). Space complexity is O(m). This feeds into L0 by being repeated k times to find the kth smallest. Common mistakes include not updating indices correctly or pushing elements when the row is exhausted. Real code pattern: heap.pop() to extract, check col+1 < len(matrix[row]), then heap.push((matrix[row][col+1], row, col+1)).

examples.trace:
Step 1: Extract min from heap: value=5, row=0, col=1.
Step 2: Check next in row 0: col+1=2, within bounds (matrix[0] has 3 elements).
Step 3: Push next element: matrix[0][2]=9 with (9,0,2).
Step 4: New heap after push: [(9,0,2), (10,1,0), (12,2,0)].

Step 1: Extract min from heap: value=13, row=1, col=2.
Step 2: Check next in row 1: col+1=3, out of bounds (matrix[1] has 3 elements).
Step 3: Do not push anything.
Step 4: New heap: [(13,2,1)].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Merging Sorted Rows Using a Min-Heap
ladders.desc: Understand how to merge multiple sorted lists (rows) by always picking the smallest available element.
ladders.explanation: The core logic involves merging m sorted rows (each a sorted list) to access elements in sorted order. We use a min-heap to track the current smallest element from each row. Initially, the heap contains the first element of each row. At each step, we extract the smallest from the heap (which is the overall smallest among all rows) and then consider the next element from that same row as a new candidate. This mimics merging k sorted lists, leveraging the fact that rows are sorted: after removing an element from a row, the next in that row is the smallest remaining in that row. The intuition is that the global order emerges by repeatedly selecting the minimum from the frontier of each row. Time complexity for merging all n*m elements would be O(n*m log m), but for kth smallest we stop early. Space is O(m). This connects to L1 as the repeated application of extraction and push. Common mistakes include not initializing the heap correctly or misunderstanding the heap order. Real code pattern: heap = [], for i in range(rows): heap.push((matrix[i][0], i, 0)).

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize heap with (1,0,0), (2,1,0), (3,2,0). Heap: [(1,0,0), (2,1,0), (3,2,0)].
Step 2: Extract min: 1, push next from row 0: (4,0,1). Heap: [(2,1,0), (3,2,0), (4,0,1)].
Step 3: Extract min: 2, push next from row 1: (5,1,1). Heap: [(3,2,0), (4,0,1), (5,1,1)].
Step 4: Extract min: 3, push next from row 2: (6,2,1). Heap: [(4,0,1), (5,1,1), (6,2,1)].
Step 5: Extract min: 4. First 4 merged: [1,2,3,4].

Step 1: Initialize heap with (10,0,0), (15,1,0). Heap: [(10,0,0), (15,1,0)].
Step 2: Extract min: 10, push next from row 0: (20,0,1). Heap: [(15,1,0), (20,0,1)].
Step 3: Extract min: 15, push next from row 1: (25,1,1). Heap: [(20,0,1), (25,1,1)].
Step 4: Extract min: 20, row 0 exhausted. Heap: [(25,1,1)].
Step 5: Extract min: 25. Merged: [10,15,20,25].

ladders.title: Building Block — Min-Heap Operations for Priority Queue
ladders.desc: Learn to use a min-heap (priority queue) to always access the smallest element efficiently.
ladders.explanation: A min-heap is a binary tree-based data structure where the parent node is always smaller than or equal to its children, ensuring the smallest element is at the root. We use it as a priority queue to repeatedly extract the minimum element in O(log n) time and insert new elements in O(log n) time. The intuition is that by maintaining a heap of current candidates, we can quickly find the next smallest without scanning all elements. In this problem, the heap stores tuples (value, row, col), and it orders by value. Key operations: push (insert) adds an element and restores heap property; pop (extract min) removes the root and restores heap property. Time complexity per operation is O(log m) where m is heap size. Space is O(m). This builds into L2 by providing the tool to merge sorted rows. Common mistakes include not using a heap library correctly or misunderstanding heapify. Real code pattern: import heapq, heap = [], heapq.heappush(heap, item), heapq.heappop(heap).

examples.input: Heap initially: [5,10,15]. Operations: push 3, then pop.
examples.output: After push: [3,10,5,15] (heap order), After pop: 3, heap: [5,10,15]
examples.trace:
Step 1: Initial heap (min-heap): [5,10,15].
Step 2: Push 3: Insert 3, heapify up: compare with parent 5, swap → heap becomes [3,10,5,15].
Step 3: Pop: Extract root 3, replace with last element 15, heapify down: compare 15 with children 10 and 5, swap with smaller child 5 → heap becomes [5,10,15].

examples.input: Heap initially: [(2,'a'), (5,'b'), (3,'c')]. Operations: push (1,'d'), then pop.
examples.trace:
Step 1: Initial heap of tuples ordered by first element: [(2,'a'), (5,'b'), (3,'c')].
Step 2: Push (1,'d'): Insert, heapify up: compare (1,'d') with parent (2,'a'), swap → heap becomes [(1,'d'), (2,'a'), (3,'c'), (5,'b')].
Step 3: Pop: Extract root (1,'d'), replace with last (5,'b'), heapify down: compare (5,'b') with children (2,'a') and (3,'c'), swap with smaller child (2,'a') → heap becomes [(2,'a'), (5,'b'), (3,'c')].

testCases.expected: After push: [7,10,9,8], First pop: 7, heap: [8,10,9], Second pop: 8, heap: [9,10]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing and Swapping Elements in a List
ladders.desc: Perform fundamental comparisons and swaps to maintain order, foundational for heap operations.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the basic operations of comparing two elements and swapping their positions in a list, which underlies heap maintenance. In a min-heap, after an insertion or deletion, we compare elements with their parents or children and swap if necessary to restore the heap property (parent <= children). The intuition is that by repeatedly comparing and swapping, we can efficiently reorganize the list into a heap structure. For example, when inserting, we compare the new element with its parent and swap if smaller; when extracting, we compare the new root with its children and swap with the smaller child. Time complexity per comparison/swap is O(1), but heap operations involve O(log n) such steps. Space is O(1) per swap. This builds into L3 as the mechanism for heap push and pop. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in indices or incorrect comparison logic. Real code pattern: if list[i] < list[parent]: swap(list[i], list[parent]).

examples.input: List: [5,10,15]. Insert 3 at end, then heapify up.
examples.trace:
Step 1: Initial list: [5,10,15]. Insert 3 at index 3: list becomes [5,10,15,3].
Step 2: Heapify up: Compare element at index 3 (value 3) with parent at index 1 (value 10). Since 3 < 10, swap: list becomes [5,3,15,10].
Step 3: Compare new index 1 (value 3) with parent at index 0 (value 5). Since 3 < 5, swap: list becomes [3,5,15,10].
Step 4: No more parents (index 0), stop. Final list: [3,5,15,10] (min-heap order).

examples.input: List: [3,5,15,10]. Extract min (remove root), then heapify down.
examples.trace:
Step 1: Initial list (min-heap): [3,5,15,10]. Extract root (value 3), replace with last element 10: list becomes [10,5,15].
Step 2: Heapify down: Start at index 0 (value 10). Compare with children at indices 1 (value 5) and 2 (value 15). Smaller child is 5 at index 1.
Step 3: Since 10 > 5, swap with child at index 1: list becomes [5,10,15].
Step 4: Check new index 1 (value 10) with children (none, as index 3 is out of bounds), stop. Final list: [5,10,15].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Sorted Lists and Basic Comparisons
ladders.desc: Learn what it means for a list to be sorted and how to compare elements to find the smallest.
ladders.explanation: This foundational level introduces the concept of a sorted list, where elements are arranged in non-decreasing order (each element is less than or equal to the next). For example, [1,3,5] is sorted because 1 <= 3 <= 5. The key operation is comparison: given two elements, we can determine which is smaller using operators like < or <=. In the context of the problem, each row of the matrix is such a sorted list. The intuition is that if we have multiple sorted lists, the smallest overall element must be at the beginning of one of these lists, because no element later in a list can be smaller than its predecessors. This simple idea leads to the min-heap approach. Time complexity for finding the smallest in m lists by scanning is O(m), but we'll improve this later. Space is O(1). This builds into L4 by providing the basis for comparing and swapping. Common mistakes include confusion about sorted order or incorrect comparison logic. Real code pattern: if a < b: min_element = a.

examples.input: Lists: [2,5,9] and [3,7,10]. Find the smallest overall element.
examples.trace:
Step 1: List 1 is sorted: first element is 2.
Step 2: List 2 is sorted: first element is 3.
Step 3: Compare the first elements: 2 and 3.
Step 4: Since 2 < 3, the smallest overall is 2 from list 1.

examples.input: Lists: [1,4,7] and [1,2,3]. Find the smallest overall element.
examples.trace:
Step 1: List 1 first element: 1.
Step 2: List 2 first element: 1.
Step 3: Compare: 1 and 1 are equal.
Step 4: Both are smallest; we can pick either (e.g., 1 from list 1).

approaches.name: Binary Search on Value Range
approaches.summary: Perform binary search on the value range (min to max in matrix), counting elements less than or equal to mid using row-wise traversal to find the kth smallest. This avoids explicit sorting or heap operations.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find kth Smallest in Row-wise Sorted Matrix Using Binary Search on Value Range
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of binary search on value range with row-wise counting to find kth smallest element.
ladders.explanation: This approach leverages the sorted property of rows to efficiently find the kth smallest element without explicitly sorting all elements or using extra space. The intuition is that we can perform binary search on the possible value range (from minimum to maximum in the matrix). For each candidate value 'mid', we count how many elements are less than or equal to 'mid' using the sorted rows property. Since rows are sorted, we can traverse each row from right to left to find the count efficiently. If the count is less than k, the kth smallest must be greater than 'mid', so we search the right half. Otherwise, it's in the left half including 'mid'. We continue until left and right converge, giving us the kth smallest value. The algorithm: 1) Find min and max values in matrix. 2) Binary search between min and max. 3) For each mid, count elements ≤ mid by traversing each row from rightmost column moving left while elements ≤ mid. 4) Adjust search range based on count vs k. 5) When left == right, return left. Time complexity is O(m*log(max-min)) where m is number of rows, since each count operation is O(m+n) but n is bounded by m in worst case. Space is O(1). This connects to L1 by using the counting subroutine. Common mistakes: not handling duplicates correctly (count includes equal elements), off-by-one errors in binary search bounds, and inefficient counting that doesn't use row sorted property. Real code pattern: def kthSmallest(matrix, k): left, right = min(matrix[0][0], matrix[-1][0]), max(matrix[-1][-1], matrix[0][-1]); while left < right: mid = (left + right) // 2; count = 0; row, col = 0, len(matrix[0])-1; while row < len(matrix) and col >= 0: if matrix[row][col] <= mid: count += col + 1; row += 1; else: col -= 1; if count < k: left = mid + 1; else: right = mid; return left.

examples.trace: Step 1: Find min=1, max=15. Initialize left=1, right=15. Step 2: Binary search iteration 1: mid=(1+15)//2=8. Count elements ≤8: row0: 1,5 ≤8 → count=2; row1: none ≤8 → count=2; row2: none ≤8 → count=2. count=2 < k=8 → left=9. Step 3: Iteration 2: left=9, right=15, mid=12. Count ≤12: row0: 1,5,9 ≤12 → count=3; row1: 10,11 ≤12 → count=5; row2: 12 ≤12 → count=6. count=6 <8 → left=13. Step 4: Iteration 3: left=13, right=15, mid=14. Count ≤14: row0: 1,5,9 →3; row1: 10,11,13 →6; row2: 12,13 →8. count=8 ≥8 → right=14. Step 5: Iteration 4: left=13, right=14, mid=13. Count ≤13: row0: 1,5,9 →3; row1: 10,11,13 →6; row2: 12,13 →8. count=8 ≥8 → right=13. Step 6: left=13, right=13 → return 13.
Step 1: min=-5, max=-5. left=-5, right=-5. Since left==right immediately, return -5.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Count Elements Less Than or Equal to Target in Row-wise Sorted Matrix
ladders.desc: Implement function to count number of elements ≤ target using row sorted property.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is crucial for the binary search approach. The intuition is that since each row is sorted, we can efficiently count without scanning all elements. For each row, we find the rightmost element that is ≤ target. Because the row is sorted, all elements to the left are also ≤ target. We can use a two-pointer approach: start from top-right corner, move left if current element > target, move down if ≤ target, accumulating counts. Algorithm: 1) Initialize count=0, row=0, col=last column index. 2) While row < total rows and col >= 0: if matrix[row][col] ≤ target: count += col+1 (all elements in this row up to col are ≤ target), row++ (move to next row); else: col-- (move left in same row). 3) Return count. Time O(m+n) where m=rows, n=cols. Space O(1). This feeds into L0 by providing the count for binary search. Common mistakes: incorrect count addition (should be col+1 not col), missing the sorted property assumption, and off-by-one in loop conditions. Real code pattern: def countLessEqual(matrix, target): count=0; row=0; col=len(matrix[0])-1; while row<len(matrix) and col>=0: if matrix[row][col]<=target: count+=col+1; row+=1; else: col-=1; return count.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize count=0, row=0, col=2. Step 2: matrix[0][2]=9 >8 → col=1. Step 3: matrix[0][1]=5 ≤8 → count+=1+1=2, row=1. Step 4: matrix[1][1]=11 >8 → col=0. Step 5: matrix[1][0]=10 >8 → col=-1 (loop ends). Return count=2.

Step 1: count=0, row=0, col=2. matrix[0][2]=3 ≤5 → count+=2+1=3, row=1. Step 2: matrix[1][2]=6 >5 → col=1. Step 3: matrix[1][1]=5 ≤5 → count+=1+1=5, row=2. Step 4: matrix[2][1]=8 >5 → col=0. Step 5: matrix[2][0]=7 >5 → col=-1 (end). Return 5.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Binary Search on Value Range with Counting Condition
ladders.desc: Implement binary search that uses a counting function to narrow search range.
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique: instead of binary searching on indices, we search on the possible values. The intuition is that the kth smallest value lies between the min and max of the matrix. We use binary search to guess a value, count how many elements are ≤ that guess, and adjust the search based on whether count < k. Algorithm: 1) Initialize left=min(matrix), right=max(matrix). 2) While left < right: mid = (left+right)//2; count = countLessEqual(matrix, mid); if count < k: left = mid+1; else: right = mid. 3) Return left. Why it works: The condition count < k means the kth smallest is greater than mid, so we exclude mid and below. When count ≥ k, the kth smallest could be mid or less, so we include mid in search. This converges to the smallest value where count ≥ k, which is the kth smallest. Time O(log(range)*counting cost). Space O(1). This connects to L1 by using the counting function. Common mistakes: using left ≤ right loop causing infinite loop, incorrect update of left/right, and not handling integer division correctly. Real code pattern: def binarySearchValue(matrix, k): left=minVal; right=maxVal; while left<right: mid=(left+right)//2; if countLessEqual(matrix,mid)<k: left=mid+1; else: right=mid; return left.

examples.input: min=1, max=15, k=8, counting function gives: for mid=8 → count=2, for mid=12 → count=6, for mid=14 → count=8, for mid=13 → count=8
examples.trace: Step 1: left=1, right=15. Step 2: mid=8, count=2 <8 → left=9. Step 3: left=9, right=15, mid=12, count=6 <8 → left=13. Step 4: left=13, right=15, mid=14, count=8 ≥8 → right=14. Step 5: left=13, right=14, mid=13, count=8 ≥8 → right=13. Step 6: left=13, right=13 → return 13.

examples.input: min=1, max=1, k=1, counting function gives: for mid=1 → count=1
examples.trace: Step 1: left=1, right=1. Since left==right, return 1 without loop.

ladders.title: Building Block — Find Rightmost Element ≤ Target in Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Given a sorted array, find index of rightmost element ≤ target, or count of elements ≤ target.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is needed for the counting subroutine. The intuition is that in a sorted array, we can use binary search to efficiently find the boundary where elements become greater than target. Algorithm: 1) Initialize left=0, right=len(arr)-1, result=-1. 2) While left ≤ right: mid = (left+right)//2; if arr[mid] ≤ target: result=mid, left=mid+1; else: right=mid-1. 3) Return result+1 as count of elements ≤ target. Time O(log n), space O(1). This connects to L2 by being used per row in counting. Common mistakes: confusing ≤ with <, off-by-one in result, and not handling empty arrays. Real code pattern: def countInSortedArray(arr, target): left=0; right=len(arr)-1; idx=-1; while left<=right: mid=(left+right)//2; if arr[mid]<=target: idx=mid; left=mid+1; else: right=mid-1; return idx+1.

examples.trace: Step 1: left=0, right=2, idx=-1. Step 2: mid=1, arr[1]=5 ≤8 → idx=1, left=2. Step 3: left=2, right=2, mid=2, arr[2]=9 >8 → right=1. Step 4: left=2 > right=1 → loop ends. Return idx+1=2.
Step 1: left=0, right=4, idx=-1. Step 2: mid=2, arr[2]=3 ≤3 → idx=2, left=3. Step 3: left=3, right=4, mid=3, arr[3]=4 >3 → right=2. Step 4: left=3 > right=2 → end. Return 3.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Binary Search in Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Implement standard binary search to find target in sorted array.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is prerequisite. The intuition is dividing search interval in half repeatedly to find target efficiently. Algorithm: 1) Initialize left=0, right=len(arr)-1. 2) While left ≤ right: mid = (left+right)//2; if arr[mid] == target: return mid; elif arr[mid] < target: left=mid+1; else: right=mid-1. 3) Return -1 if not found. Time O(log n), space O(1). This connects to L3 as a base for modified binary search. Common mistakes: incorrect loop condition (use ≤ not <), miscalculating mid, and not updating bounds correctly. Real code pattern: def binarySearch(arr, target): left=0; right=len(arr)-1; while left<=right: mid=(left+right)//2; if arr[mid]==target: return mid; elif arr[mid]<target: left=mid+1; else: right=mid-1; return -1.

examples.trace: Step 1: left=0, right=2, mid=1, arr[1]=5 ==5 → return 1.
Step 1: left=0, right=4, mid=2, arr[2]=3 ==3 → return 2.

testCases.expected: 2 or 3 (first occurrence may vary, but standard binary search finds one)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Sorted Arrays and Linear Search
ladders.desc: Learn to traverse arrays and check if sorted, with basic search operations.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: understanding arrays, their order, and basic search. An array is sorted if each element is less than or equal to the next. We can check this by iterating and comparing adjacent elements. Linear search scans each element sequentially to find a target. Intuition: if array is sorted, we can stop early when we pass where target would be. Algorithm for linear search in sorted array: 1) For i from 0 to len(arr)-1: if arr[i] == target: return i; if arr[i] > target: break (since sorted, target not beyond). 2) Return -1 if not found. Time O(n), space O(1). This connects to L4 as a slower alternative to binary search. Common mistakes: not handling empty arrays, incorrect index ranges, and assuming array is sorted without verification. Real code pattern: def linearSearchSorted(arr, target): for i in range(len(arr)): if arr[i]==target: return i; if arr[i]>target: break; return -1.

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=1 !=5, 1<5 → continue. Step 2: i=1, arr[1]=5 ==5 → return 1.
Step 1: i=0, 1≠3, 1<3 → continue. Step 2: i=1, 2≠3, 2<3 → continue. Step 3: i=2, 3==3 → return 2.
